A relatively unknown or perhaps underutilized mineral like this can appreciate this much in value purely because of rising demand, but with the cherry on top, the export bans and restrictions from the country controlling the majority of the vital mineral.

It’s a very interesting mineral as it could melt in your hand but is right now used in combination with nitrogen and then used in EV fast-chargers or 5G base stations. But why the US might want a bigger piece of it has to do with its use in military radars. With the US budget potentially growing by 50% until next year, it further supports increased prices for these minerals as the money is being allocated to pay whatever the current market price is, no matter what.

E-Waste, The Place To Be?

In February 2025, MTM demonstrated ~90% and ~80% recovery rates for Gallium and Germanium respectively from semiconductor metal refining waste.

In one of the recent reports from the company, they include some figures on recent FJH performance figures. This is part of a broader e-waste business model the company is pushing. It has included figures on the economics of this, and it’s quite frankly very appealing.

20% is feedstock costs, like semiconductors and other stuff. Then there is about a 15% loss from recovery anticipated and a 15% loss in payability. But Metallium argues it can achieve a 50% gross margin on this model. The semiconductor stream is what will bring the gallium and germanium. Then you have operating and processing expenses. Metallium expects 87% lower energy consumption compared to Hydromet processing. Other advantages include 37% higher yield per tonne along with 69% lower input costs compared to Hydromet plus remote mining.

But the story here is not just about these two items. Yes, they are big ticket items, but when the company also reports a 100% recovery in Gold and Titanium, or 97% for Silver and Tin then you have to realize this: it's not just about rare earth minerals anymore, but instead about being a leading recovery company offering diversification away from China, and instead into the arms of the US.

Mining Exploration

The mining exploration part of Metallium concerns in large parts, mostly the Australian footprint it has, then anything else really. It’s not a story about rare earths here, something which Australia has plenty of in terms of identified reserves. The country ranks fourth with 3.8 million tonnes found so far. Rather, the headline should be the Niobium. The mineral concerns the West Arunta project, which is directly adjacent to WA1 Resources. This company made one of the biggest discoveries of the precious minerals in the past 70 years.

The mineral is used to solve the bottleneck in steel production, helping make it either stronger or lighter basically. Now, Metallium has not yet found this yet at their project, but being so close, basically a neighbour to one that did unarguably made their chances just so much better. It's only my opinion that Metallium could possible mine the mineral here, but I have to make it clear they have not yet found any.

If Metallium announces it has found carbonatite which is the rock that holds the metal, then this segment of the business might be what makes the stock shoot up even more. It could trump the processing side if the quantity is high enough. The price has since 2021 increased by about 62%. It’s not the crazy growth that REE has seen, it is still considerable.

Glencore De-Risking

When I say de-risking I mean it. The recently signed agreement with Glencore makes the ambitious plan of setting up large recovery facilities across the US over the long-term a reality, and most definitely ensures that the short-term goals will be met when talking about the first Texas facility commissioning.

This multi-year contract guarantees access to 2,400 tonnes per annum of electronic scrap, providing the feedstock certainty needed to commission and scale the company's revolutionary Flash Joule Heating (FJH) technology in Texas.

The 8,000 in nameplate capacity that Metallium has been aiming for in Q3/Q4 of this year just secured about 30% of its feedstock with this deal. The business falls apart if it can’t get this feedstock into the plant and start generating revenues. Important to know is that the contract will be running until the end of this year. It’s not a multi-year deal, but a way to secure feedstock now in the initial start-up phase, but I would guess with a lot of potential to be renewed if Metallium is able to execute its plan this year.

With feedstock secured, the only other question you should have is “what about the funding?”. Well, Metallium has got this sorted out as well. Back in December 2025, it secured a partnership with ElementUSA which includes $10.1 million which is non-dilutive as well. I find this dilution part extremely important seeing as we are so close to actually starting to produce revenues, and a massive share count increase prior to this could sour the market mood. The company also has about $50 million cash in the bank as well. Combined, the $60.1 million or so here is enough to cover operating costs ($19.9 million TTM) for the next 3 years. If based on the operating cash flow and capital expenditures ($3.1 million and $600,000) the cash covers the operations for a longer period, 16.2 years to be specific. This gets us well within when Metallium should actually start producing revenues and scale up to 8,000 tonnes per year.

Revenue Model

So effectively meaning that of the contained metal value, we're aiming to capture 40% of that to our bottom line.

This quote comes directly from the CEO Michael Walshe when the company presented at one of Morgan Stanley's events back in November. No doubt the market is excited when a mining company comes out and says they are aiming for 40% net margins when others like MP or Lynas Rare Earths Limited (LYSCF) have a hard time even being profitable. MP, for instance, has reported a net loss for over 2 years straight and Lynas reported just above $5 million in net income with $366 million in revenues on a TTM basis.

Seeing as the company is still pre-revenue, I think the most reasonable approach is to make a “waterfall” approach here for the revenues and earnings. Essentially, we are looking at the input cost which, in this case, is “metals of board” using a $28,000 price per tonne here. This table is highly diverse, meaning it basically uses a combined yield rather than breaking out specifics, like 100% for gold and titanium.

It’s impossible to know the actual mix of what feedstock Metallium will be using, so this does act as a very nice way of gauging the revenue potential. Speaking of this, based on the 8,000 tonne target the annual operating income would be $145 million and $80.1 million in net margins. For a company with an EV of $385 million it does suggest there is a lot of upside potential here in the short to medium term just based off of this.

This table here instead looks at the semiconductor scraps which would start in Q3 at the same time as the line for Gallium and Germanium would start. Because of the higher concentration of these in semiconductors the table changes dramatically and so does the revenue profile, $362 million in operating income and $272 million in net income. This, of course, is at the moment quite unlikely as getting 8,000 tonnes per year of this stuff is hard, and it’s not just Metallium recycling, there are others. But it does show the impact that appreciating mineral prices has on the net income as much of the potential growth of those two, or any other for that matter, almost directly hits the bottom line of Metallium.

Metallium will, of course, not be operating with that capacity of 8,000 tonnes straight out of the gate. Just based on the first table and the secured feedstock from Glencore implies $43.6 million in operating income and $24.03 million in net income. I’m very comfortable buying this at 20x earnings because of the growth implications here. This is even below the broader Materials sector it’s in. At 20x the estimated earnings figure I arrive at a market cap of $480 million. I won’t make any models here pointing to a billion dollar valuation, I think it’s too early. But I would lie if I said I don’t believe it could ever reach that, and fast too.

Downside Risk

The main downside risk for Metallium and most of the mining companies I’ve covered which are pre-revenue is bottlenecks in the construction phase. Any slowdowns or setbacks is going to remarkably hurt the stock price in the short term and create a lot of volatility. Those who have held the stock since a year ago wouldn't care too much about this considering how it has moved, but for newer investors it's a risk worth considering. This can then carry over into more share dilution as equity is needed to fund the operations.

The second risk is how tied the stock price of Metallium will be to commodities. Changes in global exports and bans like what China has done is going to have a more noticeable impact as well. This outsources the risks so to speak, and that is not always a good thing. It means the company has less control of performing well financially in simple terms. It has less control of what the net earnings for a certain quarter would be. That will be set by whatever the net selling price of their end products are, and there Metallium has little bargaining power, it’s whatever the market says it is.

The Full Picture

Metallium is a very exciting play right now as a mineral processing company showcasing industry-leading yields and low operating costs as well. The stock has been on an absolute surge the past 12 months but still shows upside based on my relatively conservative estimates of $480 million market cap. Its financials are in order with $50 million in cash and $10.1 million in secured non-dilutive loans. It’s a Strong Buy. This is a long-term investment based more on changing REE processing away from China and into the west, whilst benefiting from higher commodity prices.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: How Inspiring New CEO Will Take Fight To Key Rival Eli Lilly In 2026
data.description:
Investment Overview

Last night, I sat down and read through the transcript of Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) CEO Maziar Doustdar's "fireside chat" at the JP Morgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference on January 13th. Having listened to it again today, I am convinced, firstly, that it may be the most impactful interview given by any Pharma CEO in 2026, and secondly, that it will have a profound impact on Novo's share price and valuation in 2026.

In this article I am going to explain why.

Background To Novo, Semaglutide, & Pharma's "Annus Horribilis" In 2025

In February last year, I purchased stock in Novo, when it was valued at ~$85 per share. I then watched on in disbelief as it plunged in value, reaching a low of ~$45 per share in early August - a loss of of >45%.

It was Novo stock's lowest ebb for four years, but between August 2021 and August 2024, stock rose in value from $45, to $140 - up >210%. This was largely due to the runaway success of a single drug molecule - semaglutide.

Semaglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist , approved to treat patients with Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D") in December 2017, brand name Ozempic, and then approved to treat patients with obesity in June 2021, brand name Wegovy.

The drug is famed for its ability to help patients lose weight - >15% at 72 weeks at the lower dose, and using the more recently tested higher dose (7.5mg), >20% at 72 weeks. Prior to the arrival of semaglutide, the scientific community had not believed that such dramatic weight loss was achievable, but the drug's unique abilities to stimulate insulin release, reduce glucagon, slow stomach emptying, and decrease appetite, have proven ground-breaking.

In 2022, Ozempic earned revenues of ~$8.3bn, and Wegovy ~$860m. In 2023, Ozempic revenue grew to ~$13.3bn, and Wegovy revenues to ~$4.4bn. In 2024, the figures were ~$16.8bn and ~$8bn, and in 2025, although final figures have not been shared, based on performance to the end of Q3, I'd estimate Ozempic will earn ~$19.6bn of revenues, and Wegovy ~$11.8bn.

Together, these two drugs will account for ~66% of Novo total revenues (which I estimate will be ~$48bn) in 2025. Wegovy sales will be up ~45% year-on-year, and Ozempic sales up ~17%. Novo's total revenues will be up ~18% year-on-year.

Given these numbers - and analysts (credible, in my view) estimates that semaglutide could achieve close to $60bn in peak annual revenues in 2031, readers may be asking "crisis, what crisis?". How does a Pharma growing revenues at >15% per annum, while marketing and selling what may be the world's best-selling prescription drug, lose ~65% of its share price value in less than 12 months?

I have discussed the reasons why in previous posts on Novo. The underwhelming performance of another GLP-1 drug class candidate, Cagrisema, in clinical studies is one. The imposition of tariffs on imports into the U.S. announced by President Trump last April another, and the ongoing campaign to introduce a "most favored nation" ("MFN") pricing policy in the U.S., and impose tariffs specifically on pharma imports a third.

A fourth, and possibly most important factor - Novo's rivalry with Eli Lilly (LLY) and its tirzepatide molecule - another member of the GLP-1 drug class, approved to treat T2D as Mounjaro, and obesity as Zepbound, with weight loss data just as impressive (and perhaps even marginally superior to) semaglutide.

Then there was downgraded guidance (when announcing Q2 earnings), compounded drug manufacturers making "knock-off" versions of semaglutide and exploiting regulatory loopholes to sell them via platforms such as Hims and Hers (HIMS), and the unexpected departure of the Pharma's longstanding CEO, Lars Fruergaard Jorgensen, in May.

Why I Am So Energized By New CEO's Comments At JPM Healthcare Conference Part 1 - Narrowing The Focus

Into the vacant CEO position stepped Maziar Doustdar, who, according to his Wikipedia profile, "joined Novo Nordisk in 1992 as an office clerk in Vienna."

It seems very clear to me that for a company with Novo's credentials to have lost 65% of its share price value in less than 12 months, management must have been doing a lot of things wrong, and during his recent fireside chat several things struck me about the approach of the new CEO that I found immediately impressive / intriguing.

First of all, one thing Novo failed to do (in my view) in 2024 and 2025 was to control the narrative and hype around the GLP-1 drug class and its potential. While analysts shared forecasts for a >$125bn market by 2029, and hype around GLP-1 drugs reached fever pitch, Novo committed a cardinal sin by over-promising, and under-delivering.

In my view there was no need to promise the market that Cagrisema would achieve >25% weight loss, when the figures achieved by semaglutide were already impressive enough, yet that it was Novo did. When Cagrisema came up short, the market had a legitimate reason to dump Novo stock.

Consider these comments from the new CEO, however:

Novo has a 103-year history. And then of course, we are mainly synonymous for doing a couple of things, but doing it better than anyone else. The company has always been about diabetes and remains so. We have more recently moved into the obesity space innovating in that front, and that is also remains very much intact.

Then, of course, when you take the 2 large chronic disease of diabetes and obesity, you recognize that patients suffering from these conditions often have many other issues, comorbidities related to them, chronic kidney disease, CVD, MASH and the likes.

And we have been moving into these adjacencies the last few years more and more. And then we will continue doing that. But with one fine difference, I would say, and that is that the starting point for us will have to be focused around a patient, that's either suffering from high blood sugar, so diabetes or high weight obesity.

In summary, I believe the CEO is saying "let's not focus on what we don't do and the drugs we don't yet sell, let's focus on what we are doing with the drugs we do sell, because we are doing it better than anyone else".

To put it another way, why should Novo's valuation be based around the potential of Cagrisema, when the company has semaglutide, a drug whose phenomenal progress drove a >200% gain in its share price between 2021 and 2024?

If Novo earns ~$48bn of revenues in 2025 as per my estimate, forward price to sales ratio will be ~5.6x, and if we take nine-month earnings per share ("EPS") of $2.7, and average out for the full year - ~$3.6, we arrive at a price to earnings ("P/E") ratio of ~17x.

Now let's compare this to Eli Lilly, which has a market cap valuation of $985bn at present, a trailing 12m P/S ratio of ~16x (based on full-year revenue forecast of ~$63bn), and P/E ratio of ~51x.

Yes, Eli Lilly has a wider and more diverse product range than Novo, but make no mistake, its share price gains of >400% on a five-year basis have been driven almost exclusively by tirzepatide, or Zepbound / Mounjaro, a drug that is far more similar to semaglutide than it is differentiated.

For anybody who believes narrowing the focus at Novo may result in lower revenue growth / overdependence on obesity / T2D, CEO Maziar Doustdar's take is as follows:

For Novo Nordisk, we have realized that we are really good when we focus. So the focus, again, will go back to obesity and diabetes. And for those who then will get a little bit nervous, just a couple of things. I'll remind them that those couple of things have 2 billion people suffering from those conditions. So this is a very, very large unmet need where we and our competition today are only touching the surface when it comes to treatment of these individuals.

Why I Am So Energized By New CEO's Comments At JPM Healthcare Conference Part 2 - Admitting Mistakes, Making Changes

CEO's at large companies typically do not like to admit to making mistakes - all too often, it will result in their being relieved of their jobs, but in CEO Doustdar's case, there is a unique opportunity for someone who started out as an office clerk to admit some culpability, clear the decks, and adjust the strategy.

Here is what the CEO had to say about Novo's disastrous 2025 during his fireside chat:

So first, I'll come up with not an excuse, but a bit of a defense on 2025. '25 was a difficult year. It was difficult for Novo Nordisk, has been difficult for our shareholders. So I will not belittle that one bit.

But I will say, it's the curse of a leader. We went into obesity some 30 years ago. And at the time -- and up until more recently, I would say, many of our peers that have sat in this stage now or will sit here in the future were making fun of us.

They basically thought obesity is not a disease. Obesity, there is no money in it. Obesity is something that should be left to individual patients to get on with it, get on with your lifestyle, get on with your exercise and diet and kind of -- this is not for pharma to go there.

To me, this take feels entirely right. Given Novo was largely responsible for creating today's >$60bn per annum GLP-1 drug market, perhaps the company deserves more credit from Mr. Market, especially given it also continues to shape the future of this industry?

However, the new CEO is also humble enough to admit that Novo cannot afford to rest on its laurels:

we're no longer on our own, and we need to hurry up and it cannot be like the previous couple of years where we were left alone just because we had a head start.

On the hindsight, when I look back, we could have done better. And it's easy for those who are following us to correct our mistakes and make sure that they do a better version of it.

The CEO then goes on to discuss the promotion of Wegovy / Ozempic, admitting to some hair-raising oversights:

to hear 55 million people have insurance was really exciting. Only then to get into the details and realize that's a bit theoretical. All the pre-authorizations, the obstacles that the insurance companies have created for many of these millions means that it's not that they're going to be able to get their medicine just because they're obese and/or have insurance.

Doustdar is admitting to a misjudgment - Novo believed all it had to do was manufacture and ship Wegovy / Ozempic, and patients would flock to buy it, while insurers would make it easy for them to do so.

We now know that is not the case - the cash market may emerge as king, fueled by DTC platforms - similar, in fact, to those platforms that were so successful making compounded semaglutide available to cash buyers in 2025.

The CEO told the audience on Jan 13th:

We have relaunched our NovoCare Pharmacy on the back of the, what you call, the Wegovy pill. I'm very optimistic about that, but we also realize that that's not enough future. We need to meet the patients where they are. So we have gone into a massive amount of partnerships with Ro, LifeMD, Amazon, WeightWatchers, Costco and really making sure that we make our products available.

Us and Lilly combined have probably 10 million, 15 million patients. What about the other 85 million, we need to get to them.

Why I Am So Energized By New CEO's Comments At JPM Healthcare Conference Part 3 - Taking On Lilly, It's Biggest Rival

While Novo stock plunged in 2025, and the Pharma became engulfed in crisis, Lilly's progress was serene - shares gained ~40% in 2025, and it became the first trillion-dollar valuation Pharma. By my calculation, tirzepatide will earn revenues of ~$35bn in 2025, making it the world's best-selling prescription drug - but only narrowly ahead of semaglutide.

Now, let's consider the competitive landscape in 2026. Importantly, both Lilly and Novo have secured drug pricing deals with the Trump Administration (see my November 2025 note for more detail), agreeing to drop the prices of Ozempic / Wegovy and Zepbound / Mounjaro from ~$1.3k per month, to ~$350 (if purchased via Trump Rx, although I'd expect Novo's DTC channel to offer a similar price).

Crucially, however, Novo has made it to market first with an oral version of the self-injectable Wegovy, which was approved on 22nd December 2025, while Lilly's approval decision for its oral GLP-1, orforglipron, has been delayed, from 28th March, to April 10th.

Furthermore, oral Wegovy arguably looks like the superior drug - the pill showed a mean weight loss of 16.6% in its pivotal OASIS 4 trial, while orforglipron achieved a figure of 7.9%. Yes, oral Wegovy will be sold at a discount - ~$145 for non-insured patients, and as low as $25 for insured patients - but the market is so vast, revenue growth remains the logical outcome.

Back to CEO Doustdar's fireside chat, and I love the fight and attitude shown when discussing oral Wegovy versus the competition. Firstly, on tolerability:

Then when you look at it from a tolerability point of view, when you look at our clinical trials, some 7% of the trial participants left us due to tolerability issues. When you look at my competitors, it was around at the highest dose from 22% to 24% that left.

On the potential need for patients to fast prior to taking oral Wegovy:

we have about 1.5 million patients to the last count I had, on Rybelsus, which is the same drug in the diabetes segment. And we have not seen this being an issue with 1.5 million people. So I beg to think that this should be the same with Wegovy pill.

And on injectables versus orals:

My father in law has been very much waiting for an oral, even though he has been needing and wanting to take a GLP-1. The reason he actually did not want to go for Wegovy has been, he didn't feel like he should get an injection. I think there's a societal taboo on injection.

There's also the element of refrigeration. I have a friend in Montana, who basically is -- my competition's drug and mentioned to me the other day that wants to switch to Wegovy pill. And I have to say I was surprised. Why do you want to do that? And I first thought, it was just because he's my friend. But that was not the case. This gentleman travels a lot. And he's simply too nervous about all the time having kept the medication in cold chain.

Firstly, these are important and accurate points, and secondly, I cannot help but admire the subtle swipes taken against Novo's biggest (and in fact, at present, only) competitor - the almighty Eli Lilly.

Finally, on the potency of semaglutide versus tirzepatide and the use of larger doses:

our company is also a bit conservative when it comes to these things. We really feel that we should not overdose patients unless -- it's just -- so we thought 2.4 milligrams, 15%, 16%, 3x Saxenda, fantastic. We went in that direction and the product came out. And we then did a lot of studies around heart, kidney and took it from a very much diabetes muscle memory that we had and developed semaglutide and brought it to the market.

I cannot speak to what my competition has done. I can only imagine that when you are second to market and you're designing your trials, then you look at whatever is existing. So let's assume that Novo Nordisk product has 15%, 16%, and you want to get to 20%, 21%, then you tell yourself well, I've got to go all the way up to 15-milligram of my product to get there and then you design your trials and then you show that you're efficacious.

The world, in my opinion, has a little bit misread this.

The world, in my opinion, has a little bit misread how to value Novo Nordisk's business, but I expect this to change in 2026.

Concluding Thoughts: A Bullish New Approach From Novo, But Can It Drag Share Price Out Of The Doldrums?

This is the million, or rather $1 trillion, dollar question - you have two companies, Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk, built on the strength of their diabetes / obesity franchises, duking it out for market supremacy.

Internationally, Novo claims to have ~68% of the market, while in the U.S., Lilly claims to have nosed ahead. In that sense, the fact that Lilly is worth >3x more than Novo in terms of market cap feels almost laughable, however I have cautioned before that the market is rarely rational, and arguably, Lilly's share price can keep growing.

Lilly arguably has the lead in terms of manufacturing, committing to spend ~$27bn in the U.S. - although in a post President Trump world, if international tariffs are removed, manufacturing in the US may prove to be a costly error.

Novo, meanwhile, has been in a mess operationally, but has reacted by appointing a new CEO, and the signs are this new boss is not at all the same as the old boss.

President Trump arguably rose to prominence in the U.S. political world thanks to his ability to deliver hard truths, highlighting what he felt was wrong with economy, international relations etc. and explaining how he would fix it, and not being afraid to take on his rivals head-on, as opposed to obliquely.

While I would not remotely compare Novo's new CEO with the U.S. President, it does strike me that Doustdar is not afraid to criticize his own company's performance if necessary, to pick fights with his rivals, and to focus on what "makes Novo great," rather than concerning himself with what may make it not so great.

It's a refreshing attitude that immediately struck me as I went through the transcript from the JP Morgan conference, and arguably, much-needed after the debacle that was the first eight months of 2025 for the company.

It is, however, a double edged sword. Making enemies within you own company by criticizing past performance is not always wise, and neither is admitting to mistakes, i.e., Novo's lack of understanding of the U.S. insurance industry. If you made that mistake yesterday, what stops you making it tomorrow?

Nevertheless, I found many of the new CEO's arguments persuasive. Oral Wegovy is best-in-class and reached >3k patients in its first week on the market. Novo needed to adjust its marketing strategy and has done so.

Novo will report earnings and likely provide 2026 guidance on February 4th, and I'd expect management to forecast another year of double-digit revenue growth, and EPS growth, and I am confident that will be a catalyst for share price gains, as will the performance of oral Wegovy and pipeline progress - let's not forget, Novo is the original, and arguably still the best at GLP-1 drug development.

As such, I believe it would not be unrealistic to see stock mount a recovery and trade somewhere between $75 - $100 by year end. That represents upside at the midpoint of >30%, running ahead of what I'd expect to be ~10-15% annual revenue / EPS growth, but, starting from such a low ebb, I'd expect share price gains to run ahead of company performance - provided the new CEO can truly steady the ship operationally and demonstrate his commercial savvy.

The company is right to focus on its core strengths of diabetes and now obesity (if you want to read an excellent analysis of how Novo may augment this strategy with strategic M&A, I'd refer you here), given this market, despite already being the biggest in pharmaceutical history, is still in its infancy.

Luckily, in the obesity space, it is not the case that you must buy Novo and sell Lilly, or sell Novo and buy Lilly. You can own both, and they may even be good for one another, as Pepsi's rivalry with Coke is arguably good for both companies, for example.

Obesity / GLP-1 drugs was the biggest pharmaceutical story of 2024, and 2025, and I fully expect it to be the biggest pharmaceutical show in town in 2026, also. Under its new CEO, I expect Novo to take the fight to Lilly in way it was unable to do last year. In short - let's get ready to rumble!
